s: A museum that isn't quite a museum
w: Founded 1988, it claims to be 'dedicated to the advancement of the Lower Jurassic' — a phrase whose meaning is never explained. Exhibits include a bat that flies through solid walls and a trailer park in the Mojave. Somewhere between sincerity and hoax, and the boundary is the whole point. MacArthur genius grant, 2001.
_facts: Founded 1988 by David and Diana Drake Wilson on Venice Blvd
Officially dedicated to 'the Lower Jurassic' — term never defined
Exhibits blend authentic artefacts with plausible fiction
MacArthur Fellowship awarded to David Wilson 2001

s: An 1893 atrium said to be conjured via planchette
w: Mining millionaire Lewis Bradbury hired draftsman George Wyman, who agreed — legend says — only after consulting his dead brother via Ouija board. The result: five storeys of ornate ironwork under a skylit atrium, doubling as the future in Blade Runner and a dozen noirs. Working office building since 1893.
_facts: Built 1893, designed by George Wyman from plans by Sumner Hunt
Wyman supposedly consulted his dead brother via planchette before accepting the commission
Featured in Blade Runner (1982) as Tyrell Building interior
Commissioned by gold-mining millionaire Lewis L. Bradbury

s: World's shortest railway, three closures and counting
w: Two funicular cars named Olivet and Sinai have climbed this 298-foot track since 1901 — with gaps for Bunker Hill redevelopment, a fatal derailment in 2001, and mechanical failures. The original line was dismantled in 1969, stored, then reassembled one street south. Still $1 a ride.
_facts: Opened 1901, cars named Olivet and Sinai on shared cable
Track is 298 feet covering 96-foot vertical gain
Closed 1969 for Bunker Hill redevelopment; reopened 1996 at current location
Fatal accident in 2001 led to a further closure until 2010

s: Seventeen towers built alone, by hand, over 33 years
w: Sabato Rodia — an Italian immigrant tile mason — built all 17 towers solo from 1921 to 1954 using steel rods and whatever he could press into cement: sea shells, broken china, green 7-Up glass. Tallest reaches 99 feet. When finished he handed the property to a neighbour and left. No plans, no commission.
_facts: Built solely by Sabato Rodia from 1921 to 1954
17 interconnected towers, tallest at 99.5 feet
Materials include seashells, broken pottery, and bottle glass embedded in cement
Rodia deeded the property to a neighbour and departed permanently upon completion

s: The grandest movie palace on Broadway, opened for Chaplin
w: Opened January 1931 for the world premiere of City Lights: 2,000 seats, five-storey French Baroque lobby, cascading chandeliers, children's playroom. The Depression killed foot traffic almost at once; it has spent most of its life as a film-shoot location. Arguably the most ornate cinema ever built in California.
_facts: Opened January 9, 1931 for world premiere of Chaplin's City Lights
2,000 seats with five-storey French Baroque lobby
Designed by architect S. Charles Lee
Spent much of its life as a film shoot and special-event venue

s: Wright's first LA commission, for an heiress who never moved in
w: Oil heiress Aline Barnsdall commissioned Frank Lloyd Wright in 1919, argued through construction, and never lived there. Every surface is stamped with stylised hollyhock, her favourite flower. Wright's opening move in Los Angeles; now a UNESCO World Heritage Site on a hill in East Hollywood that the city got for free.
_facts: Designed by Frank Lloyd Wright for Aline Barnsdall, completed 1921
Named for hollyhock-inspired motifs throughout the structure
Barnsdall donated the property to the City of Los Angeles; never occupied the house
UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2019 as part of Wright's 20th-century architecture

s: Mayan Revival in concrete block; the building Blade Runner borrowed
w: Wright used 27,000 patterned concrete textile blocks for this hilltop house in 1924 — largest of his four such LA homes. It appears in Blade Runner, The Day of the Locust, and Buffy. The blocks shift as the hillside breathes; near-continuous repair since the 1994 Northridge earthquake.
_facts: Designed by Frank Lloyd Wright, completed 1925 for Charles and Mabel Ennis
Constructed from approximately 27,000 patterned concrete textile blocks
Appeared in Blade Runner (1982) as the exterior of Deckard's apartment
Listed on National Register of Historic Places; damaged in 1994 Northridge earthquake

s: Built stone by stone by a man who walked here from Cincinnati
w: Charles Lummis walked from Cincinnati to Los Angeles in 1884, filing dispatches for the Times en route. He then built El Alisal himself from Arroyo Seco boulders over 13 years, hosting John Muir and Theodore Roosevelt. The Southwest Museum he founded next door is now part of the Autry. The stone house is open weekends.
_facts: Charles Lummis walked 3,507 miles from Cincinnati to LA in 143 days in 1884
Built El Alisal from local river boulders approximately 1895-1910
Hosted John Muir and Theodore Roosevelt among other luminaries
Lummis founded the Southwest Museum in 1907, now merged with Autry National Center

s: The only hand-painted panorama built in America in over a century
w: In a converted 1920s theatre in West Adams, Sara Velas created the only hand-painted 360-degree panorama made in the US since the 19th century. Previous shows depicted the siege of Tenochtitlan. The rotunda is dark; the effect is disorienting in the best way. Admission includes the garden.
_facts: Established 2000 by Sara Velas; moved to the Union Theatre, West Adams, in 2004
Features the only hand-painted 360-degree panorama made in the US since the 19th century
Previous panoramas exhibited here include a depiction of the fall of Tenochtitlan
Name combines artist surname Velas with her mother's maiden name Asay

s: America's longest-running puppet show; birthday cake mandatory
w: Bob Baker and Alton Wood opened this in 1963 — the oldest continuously running puppet theatre in the US. Every show ends with birthday cake for any child celebrating. No exceptions in 60-plus years. The original Westlake space was lost in 2019; a Historic-Cultural Monument that relocated to York Boulevard.
_facts: Founded 1963 by Bob Baker and Alton Wood
Oldest continuously running puppet theatre in the United States
Birthday cake served after every show to any child celebrating a birthday
Designated LA Historic-Cultural Monument 2009; moved to 4949 York Blvd in 2019

s: Open since 1924, claimed never to have locked its doors
w: Claimed to have been open every hour since 1924, never locking its door — even moving day 1950: lunch in the old space, dinner in the new one. Health inspectors forced a single closure in 1997; the pandemic ended the 24-hour tradition. Cash only. Vast portions. Sourdough toast.
_facts: Opened 1924, moved to current location 1950 without closing
Claims never to have locked its doors — tradition ended with COVID-19
Briefly closed by health inspectors November 26, 1997, reopened next day
Owned for many years by former LA mayor Richard Riordan

s: Stated mission: 'to make people happy to be alive'
w: Opened 1995 with that aim. Achieved via serial-killer artwork, crime-scene photography from the Black Dahlia case, the Manson murders, and Heaven's Gate, plus mortician tools and a taxidermy room. The collection is exactly as advertised. Not recommended before lunch.
_facts: Founded June 1995 by J. D. Healy and Catherine Shultz in Hollywood
Stated institutional mission: 'to make people happy to be alive'
Collection includes serial-killer artwork, historic crime-scene photography, and embalming instruments
Black Dahlia and Manson murder artefacts are signature exhibits

s: Hollywood's oldest restaurant, where Faulkner ordered martinis
w: Open since 1919 and called 'the genesis of Hollywood.' Faulkner, Fitzgerald, Chandler, and Bukowski all wrote here or drank here or both. The martinis are poured from a cart; the flannel cakes are unchanged. Red booths, waiters in red jackets, menu essentially unchanged since the 1940s. Nothing is ironic.
_facts: Opened 1919 by Joseph Musso and Frank Toulet; oldest restaurant in Hollywood
Regular clients included Faulkner, F. Scott Fitzgerald, and Raymond Chandler
Bukowski wrote about Musso and Frank extensively
Menu largely unchanged since the 1940s; traditional red jackets on staff

s: America's first megachurch, built by a woman who faked her own kidnapping
w: Aimee Semple McPherson built this 5,300-seat Pentecostal church in 1923 — considered the first US megachurch. She broadcast on her own radio station and staged theatrical Bible dramas. In May 1926 she vanished for six weeks, reappeared claiming kidnapping; a grand jury found the charges inconclusive.
_facts: Founded 1923 by Aimee Semple McPherson, considered the first US megachurch
Capacity 5,300 seats; own radio station KFSG, one of the first religious broadcasters
McPherson disappeared May 1926, reappeared claiming kidnapping; grand jury found charges inconclusive
Still operates as International Church of the Foursquare Gospel

s: The department store designed to be entered from the car park
w: Opened 1929 as the first US store designed for motorists: the grand entrance was at the rear porte-cochere, not the street. The Parkinsons' turquoise-and-copper Art Deco tower made it the most glamorous address on Wilshire. Now Southwestern Law School; the tearoom is the moot courtroom.
_facts: Opened September 1929 for Bullock's department store chain
First US department store designed primarily for automobile access, with main entrance via rear porte-cochere
Turquoise and copper-clad Art Deco tower by architects John and Donald Parkinson
Now owned by Southwestern Law School; original tearoom serves as moot courtroom

s: Thirteen floors of turquoise and gold above South Broadway
w: Claud Beelman's 1930 masterwork for a department store chain: turquoise terracotta with gold sunburst ornament, unmissable on Broadway. The city's height limit was 150 feet; the clock tower was granted an exemption and reaches 264 feet. Converted to loft apartments in 2006; Johnny Depp reportedly occupied a penthouse.
_facts: Designed by Claud Beelman, opened September 12, 1930
Exterior clad in turquoise terracotta with gold-leaf ornament
Clock tower granted exemption from the 150-foot city height limit, reaching 264 feet
Converted to residential lofts in 2006

s: A 22-foot chicken-headed boy has watched Figueroa Street since 2007
w: Originally a mascot for a shuttered Broadway chicken restaurant, the fibreglass figure was rescued by artist Amy Inouye and stored for 20 years. In 2007 she mounted it on her Figueroa Street rooftop. Governor Schwarzenegger gave it a Historic Preservation Award in 2010. It outlives everything around it.
_facts: Fibreglass statue, 22 feet tall, originally mascot for a Broadway chicken restaurant
Rescued and stored by artist Amy Inouye for approximately 20 years
Installed on rooftop at North Figueroa Street, Highland Park, in 2007
Received Governor's Historic Preservation Award from Arnold Schwarzenegger in 2010

s: A 1927 bowling alley that ran a bootleg pharmacy upstairs
w: Opened 1927; the upstairs 'pharmacy' was reputedly a Prohibition liquor front, contraband routed through the lanes below. Renamed Mr. T's Bowl 1966, neighbourhood institution, closed 2014. Restored and reopened as Highland Park Bowl in 2016 with all original Brunswick lanes intact.
_facts: Opened 1927 as Highland Park Bowl in Los Angeles
Upstairs pharmacy reportedly a Prohibition-era bootleg liquor operation
Renamed Mr. T's Bowl 1966-2014
Restored and reopened 2016 with original Brunswick lanes

s: Lloyd Wright's 'Jaws House' — and a Black Dahlia theory
w: Lloyd Wright (Frank's son) designed this 1926 textile-block house with a facade that gapes like a shark's mouth — hence its nickname. In 2003, surgeon Steve Hodel published a book arguing his father George Hodel, who lived here in 1946-47, was the Black Dahlia killer. The claim is disputed; no charges were brought.
_facts: Designed by Lloyd Wright, built 1926 for John Sowden in Los Feliz
Facade resembles an open mouth; nicknamed 'the Jaws House'
George Hodel rented the property 1946-47; son Steve Hodel accused him of the 1947 Black Dahlia murder
The theory is disputed and no charges were ever filed

s: Depression cafeteria with fake redwoods that fed LA on the honour system
w: Clifford Clinton opened here in 1931: 'Dine free unless delighted.' His profits funded a citizens' committee that ousted corrupt Mayor Frank Shaw in 1938. The fake redwood trees, stuffed lions, and neon plants are gone — it closed as a cafeteria in 2018 — but the building and bar remain.
_facts: Founded 1931 by Clifford Clinton with the 'dine free unless delighted' honour system
648 South Broadway location had multi-storey fake redwood trees, stuffed lions, and theatrical decor
Clinton's profits funded CIVIC, which removed corrupt Mayor Frank Shaw in 1938
Closed as a cafeteria in 2018; now operates as Clifton's Republic bar

s: Built in 1818 — the oldest house still standing in Los Angeles
w: Francisco Avila built this adobe in 1818, four decades before California statehood. American forces commandeered it in 1847; Commodore Stockton used it as his headquarters. Severely damaged by the 1971 Sylmar earthquake, restored and now a free museum. Oldest building on the city's oldest street.
_facts: Built 1818 by Francisco Avila, oldest standing residence in Los Angeles
Occupied by US forces as military headquarters in 1847 during the Mexican-American War
Severely damaged in the 1971 Sylmar earthquake and subsequently restored
California Historical Landmark No. 145; free museum on Olvera Street

s: A Frank Gehry building whose entrance is a pair of 45-foot binoculars
w: Built 1991 for ad agency Chiat/Day, with the main entrance formed by a giant binocular sculpture designed by Claes Oldenburg and Coosje van Bruggen — which also functions as the parking garage ramp. Gehry wrapped the building around it. Google acquired the campus in 2011. The binoculars open at 8am.
_facts: Designed by Frank Gehry, completed 1991 for advertising agency Chiat/Day
Giant Binoculars sculpture (45 feet) by Claes Oldenburg and Coosje van Bruggen forms the facade and garage entrance
Gehry designed the building around the pre-existing sculpture concept
Google acquired the property as its Venice campus in 2011
